Q1: I tried to keep a steady routine, usually studying about 3-4 hours each day spread across the week. Being a visual learner, I'd organize my materials with color-coded notes and diagrams that I could review repeatedly. With 20 online courses to manage, I had to be pretty disciplined about blocking out specific times, though honestly some weeks were better than others. I usually studied in the mornings when my mind felt clearest.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, but realistically my pattern shifted a lot based on deadlines and how stressed I was feeling. When multiple assignments piled up, I'd abandon my usual schedule and just focus on whatever was most urgent. The stress of managing so many courses sometimes made it hard to stick to any consistent plan, even though I knew routine would help me in the long run.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something due soon or when I wanted to check if any new materials had been posted. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline and that would prompt me to open the LMS. I also logged in when I felt like I was falling behind and needed to see where I stood with my tasks. It wasn't always about active studying—sometimes just checking what I needed to do.
Q4: After the first few weeks, logging in became somewhat automatic for me, especially in the mornings with my coffee. The peer support system we had actually helped because I'd check in to see if my study partners had posted anything or if there were messages. It became part of my daily check-in routine, though I wouldn't say I spent huge amounts of time there once logged in. The habit formed mostly out of necessity with so many courses to track.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful to me because I learn best visually—I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. I also found the course slides helpful because I could screenshot them and add to my own notes. Having visual representations of concepts made everything click better for me. The peer interactions were surprisingly helpful too when I needed clarification on something I'd seen.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums except for occasional posts because typing out long responses felt time-consuming when I had so many courses. Reading materials that were just walls of text without visuals often got skipped or skimmed quickly. I knew they might be important, but with limited time and resources, I had to prioritize what worked best for my learning style. Some optional readings just never made it onto my radar.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to my exams or assignments. Visual content that I could actually learn from efficiently made me feel like the time was well spent. If something helped reduce my stress by making a confusing concept clearer, that was valuable to me. I also appreciated activities that didn't require expensive textbooks or extra resources I couldn't afford.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were like extras I'd get to if I had time, which I usually didn't. Required activities had this weight to them that made me prioritize them no matter what. With optional stuff, I'd think about whether it was really going to help my grade or understanding enough to justify the time. Sometimes I felt guilty skipping them, but I had to be realistic about my capacity.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline with downloaded lecture slides and notes I'd made by hand. I'd screenshot diagrams from videos and create my own visual study guides that I could review anywhere without internet. Sometimes I'd study from notes while commuting or during breaks at my part-time work. My physical notebooks were probably more used than the LMS itself for actual learning.
Q10: When I was creating mind maps or visual summaries from the course content, none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd spend hours reorganizing information into diagrams that made sense to me visually. Also, when I studied with my peer support group in person, we'd quiz each other and discuss concepts—that was real engagement but completely invisible online. A lot of my deepest learning happened with pen and paper.
Q11: Yes, this was my main approach honestly. I'd watch a video once or download the slides, then study from my own notes and screenshots for days afterward. It was more efficient for me to have everything saved locally where I could access it without worrying about internet or data. The LMS would only show that initial access, but the real studying happened repeatedly offline over the following week.
Q12: Around midterms when everything piled up, it became really hard to stay engaged. The stress level was intense and my motivation dropped because I felt overwhelmed by the sheer volume across all my courses. There were also times when money was tight and I was worried about basic expenses, which made it hard to focus on academics. During those periods, I just did the bare minimum to get by.
Q13: Definitely—when I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities like work and family stuff meant some weeks I barely touched the platform meaningfully. Confusion about certain topics sometimes made me avoid those modules entirely until I absolutely had to face them. The fatigue was real, especially managing 20 courses with limited resources.
Q14: There were plenty of times I'd open videos or readings and my mind would be completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about bills or work or just feeling too drained to process information. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't really learning. Those sessions probably showed as activity but they weren't genuine engagement at all.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt less urgent than assignments with hard deadlines. Some readings that seemed dense or text-heavy got pushed back repeatedly until I ran out of time entirely. I knew participating more would help my understanding, but when you're stressed and low on time, you cut what seems cuttable. The guilt was there but survival mode kicked in.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd have bursts of activity right before something was due, then quieter periods in between. The system probably showed these spikes clearly—that's just how I operated under pressure. Without a deadline looming, my motivation to log in dropped significantly because other urgent things took over.
Q17: When I got feedback on assignments, it motivated me to check my approach for the next one. Positive feedback gave me a little boost that made me want to engage more. But honestly, feedback often came too late to really change how I was studying for that particular topic. The peer support feedback was sometimes more immediate and helpful than official instructor comments.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my time, which I appreciated. Having visual content available made the structure work for my learning style. But with 20 courses, even good structure became overwhelming—there was just too much to navigate efficiently. I wished there was more flexibility in some deadline clustering.
Q19: Visual resources and well-organized modules made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when courses relied heavily on text-based content or expected expensive materials I couldn't afford. The discussion requirements felt like extra burden when I was already stretched thin. Courses that acknowledged different learning styles and resource limitations were much more accessible for me.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for information, trying to understand what's expected, or just feeling lost and clicking around without direction. High activity could also mean someone is struggling and repeatedly reviewing content without it clicking. Sometimes you're active because you're anxious about falling behind, not because you're productively working. The activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat—I performed reasonably well but my LMS activity was moderate because I did so much offline. If you learn efficiently from downloaded materials and personal notes, you don't need to be constantly logged in. Some of my best exam preparation happened completely outside the system. The grade reflected learning that happened beyond what the platform could track.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how often you log in, and what you click on. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline studying that happens. They can't measure understanding, stress levels, or whether someone is genuinely absorbing content. The numbers tell a partial story at best—they miss the human context behind the clicks.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts, and wanting to learn more. For me, it involves creating those visual summaries and discussing ideas with peers—active processing, not passive consumption. True engagement is mental investment, which doesn't always require being online.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support conversations that happened outside the platform, or the hours I spent with hand-drawn diagrams. It can't capture my stress about finances affecting my focus, or the resilience it took to keep going when motivation was low. My happiness about small victories in understanding, my offline study sessions, my visual learning strategies—none of that appears in any log. The data shows activity, not the full picture of a student trying her best with limited resources.
